answer:
**Charles Comte** and **Charles Dunoyer**, contemporaries and collaborators in the French liberal tradition, both treat *liberté* (liberty) as foundational to human rights, duties, property, and social progress, yet they define it in distinct ways that reflect their emphases: Comte on interpersonal non-interference as a precondition for rights, and Dunoyer on personal power/capacity acquired through self-overcoming.[^1][^2]

### Comte's Conception
In *Traité de la propriété*, Comte derives liberty from natural laws governing human development, positioning it as the "essential condition of the exercise of every right, and of the accomplishment of every duty."[^3] He defines it precisely as "the state of a person who encounters, **in his fellow men**, no obstacle, whether to the regular development of his being, or to the innocent exercise of his faculties."[^4] Liberty is thus fundamentally *negative*: the absence of human-imposed barriers (e.g., slavery, where one person treats another as property), enabling natural faculties to flourish without violation.[^5] Slavery negates rights/duties by reducing persons to things; liberty restores them, limited only by reciprocal respect for others' equal claims.[^6] Comte explicitly contrasts his view with Dunoyer's, noting the latter gives *slavery* and *liberty* a "sense other than that which they have in this work," referencing Dunoyer's prior *De la morale considérée dans ses rapports avec l'industrie et la liberté*.[^7]

### Dunoyer's Conception
In *Nouveau traité d'économie sociale*, Dunoyer frames liberty as emerging from industry's and morality's "influence... upon the exercise of our faculties," the "fundamental truth" that humans become "free only by becoming *industrious* and *moral*."[^8] He defines it as "*power* that man acquires to use his forces more easily *as he frees himself* from the obstacles that originally hindered their exercise," enlarging "the sphere of his action" via development (industry/knowledge overcoming ignorance/dependence on things) and moral habits (self-mastery and non-harm to others).[^9] Liberty is *positive capacity*, proportional to "industry, instruction, and... good habits"; one is "free to do only what he knows" and "with security only that which harms neither himself nor others."[^10] It grows with civilization, not birthrights, as savages lack it due to undeveloped faculties.[^11]

### Across the Two
Their meanings overlap in rejecting slavery/domination as anti-natural and emphasizing reciprocal non-harm for societal prosperity, but diverge sharply: Comte's is *interpersonal* (no obstacles *from others*, enabling rights/property); Dunoyer's is *developmental* (personal power via self-liberation from internal/natural limits, enabling faculty exercise). Comte prioritizes abolishing coercive structures first; Dunoyer sees liberty as holistic progress, with political liberty as a late "crown."[^12] The corpus contains no direct reconciliation, only Comte's footnote noting the difference; both ground it in nature's laws, tying it to property/industry against servitude. No explicit treatment appears in Comte's *Traité de législation* or Dunoyer's Vol. II.

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Preface](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/00-preface/translation/).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, metadata description links liberty to moral/industrial progress.

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chap. 3, [p-9b6829](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-9b6829).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-1c5676](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-1c5676).

[^5]: Ibid., Chap. 2 (full chapter: liberty inherent in nature's duties/rights, slavery its negation), [citation_url](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/02-de-la-liberte-consideree-comme-une-condition-de-le/translation/).

[^6]: Ibid., Chap. 3, [p-86c544](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-86c544) (principle: no legitimate possession of persons/products).

[^7]: Ibid., Chap. 3, note [^4], [p-2ffb13](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/03-de-ce-qui-constitue-la-liberte/translation/#p-2ffb13).

[^8]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Introduction, [p-67acca](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/#p-67acca).

[^9]: Ibid., Chap. 1, [p-fc8999](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-fc8999).

[^10]: Ibid., [p-583c6c](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-583c6c).

[^11]: Ibid., Introduction (full: liberty via progress across societal stages), [citation_url](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/00-introduction/translation/).

[^12]: Ibid., Chap. 1, [p-7e6539](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/01-ce-que-lauteur-entend-par-le-mot-liberte/translation/#p-7e6539) ("Political capacity is ordinarily the last that a people acquires").
